content.full_text:
ROGER A. BROWN
Lawyer
38 North Washington Street
Post Office Box 475
Sonora, California 95370
Phone (209) 533-7755                                                                           Fax (209) 533-7757

May 28, 1992


Wayne Imberi, Consultant
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: Confirmation of Advice

Dear Wayne:

     I appreciate very much your prompt and helpful advice today.  While it is still fresh in my mind, I wanted to confirm your advice in writing to make sure I understand it correctly.

     The facts are that A hired independent contractor B to perform research and analysis.  B prepared a written product.  Neither A nor B had any interest in influencing legislative or administrative action at the time the product was prepared, and it was prepared for an entirely different purpose.

     Some time later, B was hired by C to make direct contacts to influence legislative action.  B realized the written product prepared for A would be useful for the lobbying project and asked A for permission to provide the product to a legislative official.  A said there was no objection to using the product in this way.

     B was not a lobbyist or lobbying firm when the analysis, research and written product were originally prepared.  B registered as a lobbyist and lobbying firm on behalf of the new client, C.  All direct contacts and refinements of the written product after the first direct contact were paid for by C.

     Under these facts, does A become a lobbyist employer by virtue of Regulation 18614?  Is B required to report on lobbying firm reports compensation received from A for the research, analysis and written product which was ultimately provided to the legislative official?

Wayne Imberi, Consultant
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
May 28, 1992
Page Two


Your oral advice was that neither A nor B were required to report payments made or received for the preparation of the written product. You explained, after consultation with Carla Wardlaw and the Legal Division, that there is no disclosure responsibility so long as the initial intent in creating the product was not for a lobbying purpose and so long as A is not authorizing use of the product for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action.

Please confirm whether I have correctly stated your oral advice. We intend to follow your advice and would therefore appreciate your confirmation as soon as possible. Of course, if I have misunderstood your advice, please let me know at once.

Thank you, again, for your prompt and courteous responses to my questions.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Roger A. Brown

RAB/ru:211d

sections.question: Under these facts, does A become a lobbyist employer by virtue of Regulation 18614? Is B required to report on lobbying firm reports compensation received from A for the research, analysis and written product which was ultimately provided to the legislative official?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether party A becomes a lobbyist employer and whether party B must report compensation from A for research and written products that were later used for lobbying purposes, when the product was originally created for a non-lobbying purpose.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/confirmation letter from an attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. The attorney is summarizing oral advice received and requesting written confirmation. No formal CONCLUSION section exists. The letter references prior oral advice but does not contain the FPPC's written response/conclusion.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Under these facts, does A become a lobbyist employer by virtue of Regulation 18614? Is B required to report on lobbying firm reports compensation received from A for the research, analysis and written product which was ultimately provided to the legislative official?

embedding.summary: This is a follow-up letter from attorney Roger A. Brown to FPPC consultant Wayne Imberi confirming oral advice regarding lobbying disclosure obligations when a research product created for non-lobbying purposes is later used in a lobbying campaign.
